does: This paper formulates multi-turn jailbreak attacks on large language models as a reinforcement learning problem that optimizes at the trajectory level rather than individual turns. The core contribution is TROJail, which uses two process rewards—one penalizing prompts that trigger refusal mechanisms and another encouraging semantic steering toward harmful targets—to provide dense supervision for intermediate steps while directly optimizing the harmfulness of the final response. The method demonstrates improved attack success rates across multiple models and benchmarks compared to turn-level baselines, showing that trajectory-level optimization with auxiliary process rewards effectively enables learning longer-term attack strategies that avoid safety triggers while maintaining coherent attack progression.
connects: This work extends prior research on automated jailbreak generation by moving beyond turn-level optimization approaches to tackle the long-horizon planning problem inherent in multi-turn attacks. It builds on reinforcement learning frameworks for adversarial prompt generation and connects to broader work on process rewards and advantage estimation in RL, which has shown value in other domains like mathematical reasoning and code generation. The paper responds to limitations of existing attack methods that fail to learn effective multi-step strategies, and incorporates insights from safety research on how models refuse harmful requests, using this understanding to design process rewards that guide the attack trajectory while avoiding detection.
enables: This trajectory-level optimization framework opens pathways for developing more sophisticated and human-like multi-turn attack strategies that better reflect real-world adversarial scenarios. The process reward design approach could be extended to other jailbreak modalities beyond text or combined with other auxiliary signals to further improve attack success. More broadly, the work enables deeper investigation of model vulnerability to coordinated multi-turn manipulation, which should inform better defenses and alignment techniques. The RL formulation and process rewards could also drive development of more effective safety training methods that account for long-horizon adversarial interactions, and potentially inspire similar trajectory-level optimization approaches for evaluating and improving robustness in other multi-turn model behaviors.
